blurb: A tough shortgrass-prairie native whose stiff stems light up late summer with crowded spikes of rosy-purple bottlebrush flowers — a butterfly magnet anchored by a deep taproot that laughs at drought.
water: Low; xeric — deep taproot, very drought-tolerant
spread: Clumping from a woody taproot; reseeds
seasons: Rosy-purple flower spikes late summer–fall; fine grassy foliage; tan seedheads persist into winter
wildlife: Top butterfly nectar plant; native bees; seeds eaten by birds

shots.cap: Fall — flower spikes · © Bridget Sandoval (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — whole plant in bloom · © David Martin (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Fall — in shortgrass prairie · © Nathan Kelbe (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: A tough, long-lived prairie native of the Great Plains whose range runs north into the cold Canadian prairie, so it sails through Boulder winters (zone 5b–6a) with no protection.[2] The deep taproot makes it the most drought-tolerant of the gayfeathers and well-suited to our semi-arid ~15-inch climate.[1]
care.planting: Set out container-grown plants in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, while soil is workable; water in to establish, then taper off. Because of the deep taproot, plant small/young stock rather than large divisions, and site it permanently — it resents being moved. In pots it needs a gritty, fast-draining mix and protection from winter wet, which rots the corm-like rootstock.[1]
care.sun: Full sun — it wants the open, unshaded exposure of its native shortgrass prairie, and our intense high-altitude sun is no problem.[1]
care.soil: Lean, well-drained, sandy or gravelly, often calcareous soils; it is tolerant of the alkaline clay common around Boulder so long as drainage is sharp. Avoid rich, amended, or heavy wet ground, which rots the taproot.[1][2]
care.water: Low; xeric once established. The long taproot makes it exceptionally drought-tolerant — give occasional deep soakings the first season, then little to no supplemental water.[1]
care.spacing: Allow about 2 feet between plants in a border.[1]
care.propagation: Easiest from seed; the deep taproot makes mature plants very hard to move, so divide only with care. Scarified seed can be sown outside in late fall, or cold-stratified ~3 months at ~40°F and sown the following spring. Established clumps can also be increased by dividing the corm-like rootstock in late fall to early spring, but expect transplant resentment from the taproot.[1]
care.sow: Sow scarified seed outdoors in late fall to let winter provide stratification, or stratify ~3 months at 40°F and sow in spring.[1]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify about 3 months (roughly 90 days) at ~40°F to break dormancy.[1]
care.bloom: Blooms late summer into fall — roughly August through October — with rose-purple flower wands.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low care once established; leave the seedheads for winter structure and birds, and avoid overwatering or fertilizing, which causes floppy growth and rot.[1]
care.troubles: Root rot in wet, poorly drained, or over-irrigated soil is the main risk; otherwise largely pest- and disease-free.[1]

references.name: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Liatris punctata
USDA NRCS Plant Guide — dotted gayfeather (Liatris punctata)

edible.summary: No documented culinary use; grown as an ornamental and pollinator plant, not for eating.[1]
edible.caution: Liatris punctata has only documented medicinal/traditional uses, not food uses, so it is treated as inedible here — don't eat any part. It is not notably poisonous, but absence of food documentation is not a green light.[1]
